text: Over the past forty years I've been loaded on booze, coke, acid, Quaaludes, glue, cough mixture, heroin, Rohypnol, Klonopin, Vicodin, and too many other heavy-duty substances to list in this footnote. On more than a few occasions I was on all of these at the same time.
speaker: Ozzy Osbourne
context: From a footnote in his 2010 autobiography 'I Am Ozzy' (with Chris Ayres), cataloguing four decades of his own substance abuse.

source.title: Memoir 'I Am Ozzy' (LA Weekly excerpt)

text: I left the Rocking Vicars, thinking I was going to be a star in my own right immediately. Everything was going to be wonderful and huge women would get a hold of me and do things to me with raw carrots — you know, shit like that.
speaker: Lemmy Kilmister
context: Recalling his youthful rock-star fantasies after leaving an early band (the Rockin' Vickers), in his memoir White Line Fever: The Autobiography (2002).

source.title: Memoir 'White Line Fever' (The Current review)

text: Do I think about my dick? Oh, yeah, all the time. If I think about it all the time, I got a right to sing about it. If I wasn't thinking about it all the time but thought, "It's time to write a rock song, I'd better mention my dick," then I wouldn't even be able to say "dick" right.
speaker: Iggy Pop
context: Defending the sexual content of his lyrics (the song "Trollin'" from The Weirdness) in his 2007 Rolling Stone interview.

source.title: Iggy Pop: The Rolling Stone Interview

text: I blew $20 million. I snorted my Porsche. I snorted my plane. I snorted my house in that din of drugs and booze and being lost.
speaker: Steven Tyler
context: Tallying up what his cocaine habit cost him, in his 2011 rock memoir "Does the Noise in My Head Bother You?"

source.title: Memoir 'Does the Noise in My Head Bother You?' (NME report)

text: All the drugs just neutered me. When I was on drugs, I felt like this nunnish, non-sexual person. After I stopped doing drugs I started to fuck like a bunny!
speaker: Courtney Love
context: On how getting sober changed her sex drive, in an interview with The Fix.

source.title: The Fix interview

text: I was doing a little over $1,000 a day in heroin, and then probably the same in cocaine and alcohol.
speaker: Nikki Sixx
context: Mötley Crüe bassist describing the scale of his addiction at his worst, around the period of his December 1987 heroin overdose; said in 2019 press for the Netflix film 'The Dirt'.

source.title: 'The Dirt' press (Chicago Sun-Times)

text: It's cold outside, but I'm still looking like a thotty, because a hoe never gets cold.
speaker: Cardi B
context: From a now-iconic 2014 viral Instagram/Vine clip from her pre-fame days, strutting in a bra top and skirt and explaining why she's barely dressed in winter.

source.title: Pre-fame viral clip (PAPER)

text: Little Miss Muffet sat on a tuffet, eating her curds and whey. Along came a spider, he sat down beside her, he said, "Hey, what's in the bowl, bitch?"
speaker: Andrew Dice Clay
context: A signature filthy nursery-rhyme parody from his late-'80s/early-'90s arena act; crowds would shout the punchlines back. Popularized on his stand-up specials and documented in VICE's piece on his nursery rhymes.

source.title: Stand-up act (Vice feature)

text: There are 400,000 words in the English language, and there are seven of them you can't say on television. What a ratio that is. 399,993 to 7. They must really be bad. Shit, piss, fuck, cunt, cocksucker, motherfucker, and tits.
speaker: George Carlin
context: Carlin's landmark "Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television" routine from his 1972 album Class Clown, listing the broadcast-banned words verbatim and reveling in saying them all out loud.

source.title: 'Class Clown' — Seven Words You Can Never Say on Television

text: Why me? Ten million motherfuckers freebasing, and I'm the one who blows up!
speaker: Richard Pryor
context: Pryor's frank, self-lacerating bit about the 1980 freebasing accident in which he set himself on fire, from his stand-up film 'Richard Pryor: Live on the Sunset Strip' (1982).

source.title: 'Richard Pryor: Live on the Sunset Strip' (film)

text: The ice cream man always drove an extra block away. I know he's seen us, but I think he just be in the car with his friends saying, 'Watch me how fast I make these motherfuckers run.'
speaker: Eddie Murphy
context: From Eddie Murphy's 1983 HBO stand-up special 'Delirious' (filmed at DAR Constitution Hall) — his childhood ice-cream-truck bit, where the vendor cruelly speeds off to watch the neighborhood kids chase him.

source.title: 'Delirious' (IMDb quotes)

text: My sex life is so bad, my G-spot has been declared a historical landmark.
speaker: Joan Rivers
context: Rivers being characteristically filthy and self-deprecating about her own dried-up sex life.

source.title: Stand-up one-liner (CNN roundup)

text: A couple nights ago, I was licking jelly off my boyfriend's penis. And I thought, "Oh my God — I'm turning into my mother!"
speaker: Sarah Silverman
context: From her 2005 stand-up special "Sarah Silverman: Jesus Is Magic." Her signature sweet-faced delivery lands a graphic sex image as the setup, with the punchline being the realization she's inheriting her mother's habits.

source.title: 'Jesus Is Magic' (IMDb quotes)

text: I haven't ever tried very hard. Drugs expanded my imagination and made me think outside of, what, your fucking reality. And cigarettes gave me the patience to sit and write those thoughts down in a comedy-friendly format that you could understand. And alcohol gives me the courage to stand up here in front of you judgmental pricks and do it with a quarter million dollars worth of cameras in your face.
speaker: Doug Stanhope
context: From Doug Stanhope's 2007 stand-up special "No Refunds" — his defiant, self-celebrating defense of lifelong drug, cigarette, and alcohol use as the engine of his comedy.

source.title: 'No Refunds' (special)

text: I'd sit there and smoke crack while I listened to the play. It made me fucking crazy.
speaker: Samuel L. Jackson
context: Reflecting on his 1980s/early-90s crack-cocaine addiction during his time as an understudy on Broadway's 'The Piano Lesson' (shadowing Charles Dutton), before he got sober in 1991. From a 2019 The Hollywood Reporter interview, widely reported (Entertainment Tonight, Today, People), Jan 2019.

source.title: The Hollywood Reporter interview (TODAY report)

text: Last year I was really into the word cocksucker. I thought it sounded so mean!
speaker: Danny McBride
context: Answering a rapid-fire 'favorite curse word' question in a Maxim interview promoting 'Eastbound & Down.'

source.title: Maxim interview

text: Look at your mushrooms - they look like fucking tadpoles on Viagra.
speaker: Gordon Ramsay
context: Chef Gordon Ramsay critiquing a kitchen's mushroom side dish on the "Rococo" episode of Ramsay's Kitchen Nightmares (King's Lynn restaurant), UK Channel 4, 2006.

source.title: 'Ramsay's Kitchen Nightmares' — Rococo (IMDb quotes)

text: The strangest thing I've tried to snort? My father. I snorted my father. He was cremated and I couldn't resist grinding him up with a little bit of blow. My dad wouldn't have cared, he didn't give a shit. It went down pretty well, and I'm still alive.
speaker: Keith Richards
context: Rolling Stones guitarist Keith Richards, in an April 2007 NME interview, describing the strangest thing he had snorted — his late father's cremated ashes ground up with cocaine. Reported by NME, The Hollywood Reporter, VOA, and others. Richards' manager later told NME the remark was made in jest, and Richards offered a softened account in his 2010 memoir, but he is documented giving this quote in the interview.

source.title: NME interview (later called a joke)

text: It's quite valuable to have the courage and the confidence to say, 'No, fuck off, leave me alone, thank you very much.'
speaker: Helen Mirren
context: In a 2013 Daily Mail interview, the Oscar-winning actress said that if she'd had a daughter, "fuck off" is the first phrase she would have taught her, to give her the confidence to set boundaries.

source.title: Daily Mail interview (Christian Today report)

text: I once asked a Jesuit priest what was the best short prayer he knew. He said, "Fuck it," as in "Fuck it; it's in God's hands."
speaker: Anthony Hopkins
context: Anthony Hopkins recounting a Jesuit priest's answer when he asked for the best short prayer — an anecdote he has told publicly, including on Inside the Actors Studio.

source.title: Inside the Actors Studio (IMDb quotes)

text: You've baked a really lovely cake, but then you've used dog shit for frosting.
speaker: Steve Jobs
context: Critiquing a NeXT programmer's work during a project review

source.title: Book 'The Second Coming of Steve Jobs' (Cult of Mac)

text: Maybe it was because we did it unabashedly out in the open. We didn't know it was shocking. Perhaps we were just too fucking frank.
speaker: Slash
context: The Guns N' Roses guitarist, in a Classic Rock/Louder interview, explaining why his band got a wilder reputation than peers who did 'the same shit'.

source.title: Louder (Classic Rock) — Six Pack: Slash on drugs, dying and Axl Rose

text: I think that weed kept me from wanting to kill people. And probably kept a lot of people from wanting to kill me, too — out there drunk, running around.
speaker: Willie Nelson
context: In Rolling Stone's 2019 Weed Issue cover interview, explaining how marijuana replaced his heavy drinking.

source.title: Rolling Stone — Willie Nelson: 'Marijuana Saved My Life'

text: I never took heroin, really because I'm too lazy.
speaker: Grace Slick
context: The Jefferson Airplane singer, in a Forbes interview, on why heroin never appealed to her despite her heavy drug era.

source.title: Forbes — Grace Slick Part II: 'I Never Took Heroin, Really Because I'm Too Lazy'

text: I'm in one mode right now: Entertain the shit out of people. And if people don't like it, I just don't give a fuck anymore. I just want to cut to the chase.
speaker: Bob Saget
context: In a Rolling Stone interview (republished from the vault), the 'America's Dad' comedian on his post-Full House, R-rated stand-up mindset.

source.title: Rolling Stone — A Bob Saget Interview From the 'Rolling Stone' Vault

text: I used to have a drug problem, but now I make enough money.
speaker: David Lee Roth
context: The Van Halen frontman quipping about his lifestyle, quoted in a Louder roundup of his wit (originally on Nightwatch With Charlie Rose, 1986).

source.title: Louder — The wit and wisdom of David Lee Roth

text: Hollywood is a coke town, but weed is so much better.
speaker: Miley Cyrus
context: In a 2013 Rolling Stone cover interview, contrasting cocaine with the 'happy drugs' she prefers.

source.title: Rolling Stone — Miley Cyrus on Why She Loves Weed, Went Wild at the VMAs and Much More

text: And if you can write an hour of great stuff, you're like fucking Tolstoy.
speaker: Norm Macdonald
context: In a 2011 A.V. Club interview, arguing that stand-up is the hardest writing there is.

source.title: The A.V. Club — Interview: Norm Macdonald

text: I used to have a drug problem, now I make enough money.
speaker: David Lee Roth
context: Van Halen frontman David Lee Roth's famously flip one-liner about wealth and excess, quoted in American Songwriter's roundup of his best quotes.

source.title: 15 of the Best Quotes from Van Halen's David Lee Roth (American Songwriter)

text: Alcohol was my poison. I drank Seagrams VO, a Canadian rye, with Coca-Cola. And beer. For me, beer was like air. I'd have a six-pack before noon.
speaker: Alice Cooper
context: Shock-rock legend Alice Cooper describing his heavy daily drinking before he got sober, in a Louder/Classic Rock feature on going cold turkey.

source.title: How Alice Cooper went cold turkey and made his most personal album (Louder)

text: He told me if I carried on at the same pace I'd start bleeding out of my dick, my asshole, my eyes, my nose – fucking everywhere, man. Just bleeding out of every orifice and never stopping.
speaker: Zakk Wylde
context: Black Label Society / Ozzy guitarist Zakk Wylde, laughing as he recounts his doctor's blunt warning about his extreme drinking while on blood thinners, in a 2013 Louder/Metal Hammer interview.

source.title: Black Label Society's Zakk Wylde interview: alcohol, acoustic albums and prison gigs with Ozzy Osbourne (Louder)

text: Health nuts are going to feel stupid someday, lying in hospitals dying of nothing.
speaker: Redd Foxx
context: A signature dark one-liner from the pioneering, famously raunchy stand-up comedian (star of 'Sanford and Son'), mocking clean-living health fanatics. Foxx's party-hard, cigarette-and-booze persona was legendary.

source.title: Quote Details: Redd Foxx (The Quotations Page)

text: Whiskey is a great drink. Now whiskey's very classy and adult, till you shit your pants. But other than that, it is great.
speaker: Dave Attell
context: From filthy, drink-soaked comedian Dave Attell's stand-up special 'Road Work' (2014) — Attell (the 'Insomniac' host) riffing on booze in his trademark crude style.

source.title: Dave Attell: Road Work (2014) — Full Transcript (Scraps from the Loft)

text: Cheating is a man, he sneaks out of his own house to go, like, find some happiness behind your back so your feelings aren't hurt. Cheating is for you.
speaker: Patrice O'Neal
context: From the late, notoriously unfiltered comedian Patrice O'Neal's Comedy Central special 'Elephant in the Room' (2011) — his provocative, deadpan bit reframing male infidelity as an act of consideration.

source.title: Patrice O'Neal: Elephant in the Room (2011) — Transcript (Scraps from the Loft)

text: I'm in one mode right now: Entertain the shit out of people. And if people don't like it, I just don't give a fuck anymore.
speaker: Bob Saget
context: Bob Saget — the wholesome 'Full House' dad whose real-life stand-up was famously filthy — on fully embracing his profane comedy persona, in a Rolling Stone interview.

source.title: A Bob Saget Interview From the 'Rolling Stone' Vault

text: I don't go on stage high but I'll take an edible after I get off stage and chill out. I do write a lot of jokes while high though, and then the next day I'll look at them and go, 'Well that's not that funny.'
speaker: Jim Jefferies
context: Crude Australian comedian Jim Jefferies — who built his name on drinking, drugs, and porn material — talking candidly about weed and his creative process, in a High Times interview (Aug 2023).

source.title: Jim Jefferies Is Living His Best Life (High Times)

text: I used to do drugs. I still do, but I used to, too.
speaker: Mitch Hedberg
context: One of late absurdist comedian Mitch Hedberg's most iconic one-liners, a paradoxical drug-use confession from his album 'Strategic Grill Locations' (1999). Hedberg's easygoing stoner persona and drug jokes were central to his act.

source.title: Mitch Hedberg — Wikiquote

text: I think weed is the best drug on earth. One time I smoked a joint with peyote in it, and I saw a wolf howling at the moon. Hollywood is a coke town, but weed is so much better. And molly, too. Those are happy drugs – social drugs. They make you want to be with friends. You're out in the open. You're not in a bathroom. I really don't like coke. It's so gross and so dark. It's like what are you, from the Nineties? Ew.
speaker: Miley Cyrus
context: Miley Cyrus ranking her drug preferences in her September 2013 Rolling Stone cover story, calling weed and molly 'happy drugs' and dismissing cocaine as dated.

source.title: Miley Cyrus: 'Weed Is the Best Drug on Earth' (Rolling Stone)

text: I sort of fucking woke up one day and was like, 'You're an asshole. You're not an artist,'
speaker: Lady Gaga
context: Lady Gaga recounting the self-lacerating moment she decided to quit cocaine, in a Lifetime interview ('The Conversation with Amanda de Cadenet') reported by Rolling Stone; she went on to call cocaine 'the white devil.'

source.title: Lady Gaga Reflects on Past Cocaine Use (Rolling Stone)

text: The reason we first started strumming guitars was because we wanted women and lots of them. And their daughters.
speaker: Gene Simmons
context: KISS bassist Gene Simmons, on The Oprah Winfrey Show in 1988, dismissing the idea that rock stars pick up guitars for lofty reasons — it was about groupies.

source.title: Gene Simmons Called KISS Groupie Hookups 'More Honest' Than Real Relationships (Best Life)
